one_line_takeaway: Auditing three state-of-the-art approximate machine-unlearning methods (SUNSHINE, SSD, SalUn) on DP-trained CIFAR-10/100 models with a new low-cost membership-inference auditor (A-LiRA, ~88% cheaper than online-LiRA at comparable accuracy) shows they can quietly push some retained (non-removed) samples' empirical privacy risk above the model's differential-privacy budget epsilon, even while successfully protecting the samples that were actually unlearned.
key_finding: On non-DP models, most unlearned samples become safer after unlearning but a small subset counterintuitively become more privacy-sensitive, which the authors hypothesize stems from unlearning removing memorized/duplicate features and thereby 'de-duplicating' the training set, exposing similar remaining samples (consistent with the 'privacy onion effect' of Carlini et al.). On DP models with epsilon=2 and epsilon=6, in contrast, all unlearned samples became safer (Criterion 1 satisfied) after unlearning by all three methods -- but Criterion 2 was frequently violated: all three unlearning methods (SUNSHINE, SSD, SalUn) increased the privacy risk of some retained samples above the DP budget epsilon when epsilon=2 and, to a lesser degree, epsilon=4/6, on both CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- meaning approximate unlearning can silently break the DP guarantee for data contributors who did not request removal. The effect was larger when unlearning targeted the most-risky (top-k%) samples than the least-risky (bottom-k%) ones, and shrank as epsilon was relaxed (higher DP budget). On MIA efficacy/efficiency (Table I), A-LiRA achieved AUC 0.703/TPR@1%FPR 10.3% on CIFAR-10 and AUC 0.917/TPR@1%FPR 26.8% on CIFAR-100, versus online-LiRA's AUC 0.706/TPR@1%FPR 9.7% (CIFAR-10) and AUC 0.913/TPR@1%FPR 25.4% (CIFAR-100), and offline-LiRA's AUC 0.663/TPR@1%FPR 8.6% (CIFAR-10) and AUC 0.833/TPR@1%FPR 19.3% (CIFAR-100). A-LiRA's per-sample GPU time was 0.53h (CIFAR-10) and 0.57h (CIFAR-100), versus online-LiRA's 4.76h/4.84h -- an ~88.3% time reduction cited in the abstract while matching or beating online-LiRA on TPR@1%FPR and nearly matching its AUC; offline-LiRA was fastest (0.17h/0.13h) but had the worst AUC/TPR of the three.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (stated explicitly in Section I) are threefold: (1) it re-formulates the privacy-protection success criteria for approximate machine unlearning applied to differentially private (DP) models, defining two auditing criteria based on per-sample TPR/FPR of membership inference attacks (MIAs) rather than the population-level TPR/FPR used in prior DP-auditing work; Criterion 1 requires that the privacy risk E(U_MD,X, x) of an unlearned sample x drop below E(MD, x) - t1 relative to the original model, and Criterion 2 requires that the privacy risk of every retained sample x in D\X stay at or below the model's DP budget epsilon after unlearning; (2) it introduces A-LiRA, a novel augmentation-based likelihood-ratio membership inference attack that replaces the ~512 shadow models used by online-LiRA with just one in-training and one out-training shadow model, using n data augmentations (random flip/rotate/shift) of the target sample to approximate the in/out confidence distributions as Gaussians, then thresholding the likelihood ratio of the augmented-set maximum logit-transformed confidence; and (3) an empirical audit, using A-LiRA, of three published approximate unlearning methods (SUNSHINE, SSD, SalUn) on DP and non-DP ResNet-18 models trained on CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100, showing that these methods often fail to meet the two proposed criteria -- most notably that unlearning can push some retained samples' empirical privacy risk above the DP budget epsilon, violating the differential-privacy guarantee that was originally promised to those (non-removed) data contributors.
threat_model: White-box, worst-case adversary with full access to both the original model M_D and the unlearned model U_{M_D,X}, plus complete knowledge of the training dataset D and the set of unlearned samples X. The attacker's objective is a membership-inference decision: for a given target sample x, determine whether x in D was used to train M_D (Criterion 1, on unlearned samples) or whether x in D\X was used to train U_{M_D,X} (Criterion 2, on retained samples). This is an auditing/red-teaming threat model, not a threat model for an external attacker with only black-box query access -- the paper explicitly frames the strong adversary as needed 'to assess the privacy risks associated with individual samples' via auditing, not as a realistic deployed attacker.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) their hypothesis for why some unlearned/retained samples become riskier (memorization de-duplication) is not rigorously tested and is 'left to future work'; (2) experiments are limited to CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 due to computational constraints, since privacy-risk estimation requires training 500 models per configuration, which the authors say is impractical at larger scale with current resources; (3) offline-LiRA, while much cheaper, is shown to be a weaker MIA and thus a less reliable auditing tool; (4) for non-DP models there is no theoretical upper bound on acceptable per-sample risk, so the paper proposes only an ad hoc alternative bound (Appendix A, max observed sensitivity of the original model) rather than a principled criterion; (5) only three unlearning methods (SUNSHINE, SSD, SalUn) were audited, selected as recent/best-performing rather than as an exhaustive survey of the unlearning literature.

quantitative_results: A-LiRA vs online-LiRA vs offline-LiRA on CIFAR-10: AUC 0.703 vs 0.706 vs 0.663; TPR@1%FPR 10.3% vs 9.7% vs 8.6%; per-sample GPU time 0.53h vs 4.76h vs 0.17h
A-LiRA vs online-LiRA vs offline-LiRA on CIFAR-100: AUC 0.917 vs 0.913 vs 0.833; TPR@1%FPR 26.8% vs 25.4% vs 19.3%; per-sample GPU time 0.57h vs 4.84h vs 0.13h
A-LiRA achieves an 88.3% reduction in per-sample GPU time cost relative to online-LiRA (stated in abstract/intro) while matching or exceeding it on TPR@1%FPR
Under DP training (epsilon=2 and epsilon=6), all unlearned samples from SUNSHINE, SSD, and SalUn satisfied Criterion 1 (reduced privacy risk) with no observed failures

Under DP training with epsilon=2, all three unlearning methods (SUNSHINE, SSD, SalUn) produced nonzero Criterion-2 failure rates among retained samples on both CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, for both top-k% and bottom-k% removal, with failure rates generally decreasing as epsilon relaxed from 2 to 4 to 6

Privacy-risk estimation used 500 models trained on random halves of the full training set per configuration (so each sample trained ~250 of the 500 models); A-LiRA auditing used 100 augmentations per target sample plus 30 in/out shadow models to determine the decision threshold
